items.question: Write the cross-entropy H(P, Q) and state how it relates to H(P) and D_KL(P || Q).
items.answer: H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x). H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q). When P = Q, cross-entropy equals Shannon entropy.

items.question: Why is categorical cross-entropy for one-hot labels equal to −log q(y)?
items.answer: One-hot P has p(y)=1 and p(k≠y)=0, so only the y term survives: H(P,Q) = −1·log q(y) − Σ_{k≠y} 0 = −log q(y).

items.question: Explain why minimizing cross-entropy on a fixed dataset is equivalent to maximizing likelihood.
items.answer: Empirical P from labels gives loss (1/n)Σ −log q_theta(x_i), which is negative average log-likelihood of the Bernoulli/Categorical model Q_theta. Maximizing likelihood minimizes cross-entropy.

items.question: What happens to cross-entropy as q(y_true) approaches 0?
items.answer: −log q(y) grows without bound. Gradients also blow up, which is why numerical stability (log_softmax) and label smoothing are used in practice.

items.question: When would you use binary cross-entropy instead of softmax cross-entropy?
items.answer: For binary problems or multi-label settings where labels are independent Bernoulli trials — sigmoid outputs per label with BCE. Softmax CE is for mutually exclusive single-label multi-class problems.
